title: Power Law Returns

content.statement: In many markets a few outcomes dominate the rest by orders of magnitude.
content.question: Am I treating my portfolio of bets like a normal distribution when it is really a power law?
content.mental_model: Long thin tail, most of the value sits at the front.
content.business_lens: Allow many small bets, kill mediocre ones early, double down on the few that show power-law signal.
content.life_lens: Expect power-law distributions in your projects too, most will be mid, a few will dominate.
sop: Kill bets that look mediocre quickly
Concentrate resources behind the few outliers
Reinvest winnings into more shots, not lifestyle creep
content.explainer: Sequoia’s top fund returns came from a handful of investments, most companies returned little or zero. Apple’s App Store revenue follows the same pattern. Power-law thinking changes resource allocation: spread small, then concentrate behind signal.
questions: Am I treating my portfolio of bets like a normal distribution when it is really a power law?
Of your last 20 sales or marketing pushes, which single one produced more than the other 19 combined, and are you treating it like a data point or like the answer?
Which mediocre bet are you still funding at the same level as your best one, purely because cutting it feels premature?

definitions.def: A distribution where a few outcomes drive most of the returns.

content.equation_example: Out of 15 bets this year, the top 2 outcomes generated 85% of total value. Higher concentration in the top few means your strategy should be 'find and fund the outlier,' not 'balance the portfolio evenly'; a flatter spread across many bets means you may not be in a true power-law market, or you haven't found your outlier yet.
content.closing_statement: A few outcomes will always outweigh the average one.

caption:
Principle Nº 315: Power Law Returns

In many markets a few outcomes dominate the rest by orders of magnitude. Sequoia’s top fund returns came from a handful of investments, most companies returned little or zero.

Ask yourself: Am I treating my portfolio of bets like a normal distribution when it is really a power law?
